macro_events: REGIME SHIFT — On Sun 14-Jun the US and Iran reached a deal declaring the 'immediate and permanent termination of military operations'; Trump authorized the toll-free reopening of the Strait of Hormuz and removal of the US naval blockade. Signing ceremony set for 19-Jun (Switzerland) under a 60-day ceasefire. This removes the dominant aluminum supply/risk premium — bearish for the war premium that drove the 4-yr-high rally.

Physical reality check: the deal restores Gulf shipping/logistics but not the damaged capacity. EGA's Al Taweelah still needs ~12 mo to restore regardless of the truce; ALBA remains impaired; a 3–6 mo lag separates a geopolitical resolution from actual metal supply — the structural deficit persists.

Pre-deal, aluminum had already corrected ~8% from the 02-Jun 4-yr high ($3,855 cash) to $3,536 on 12-Jun, and the cash-3M backwardation compressed from 19-yr highs (~$69 on 08-Jun) to ~$4 — acute front-end stress was easing before the truce.

Hot May US inflation (CPI +0.5% m/m, +4.2% y/y released 10-Jun; PPI +1.1% m/m) keeps the Fed hawkish (~70% odds of a Dec hike) and the USD firm (~99.9) — a headwind for $-metals; the deal's oil-price relief is disinflationary at the margin and may temper that hawkishness over time.
China unwrought aluminum + semis exports +15.5% YoY to 632,000 t in May (5-mo cumulative ~2.69 Mt, +10.4% YoY) — adds ex-China supply/competition.
lme_commentary: Commentary: The confirmed official series runs through 12-Jun ($3,536 cash), pre-dating the 14-Jun US-Iran deal that reopened the Strait of Hormuz and lifted the US naval blockade. With the dominant war premium now unwinding, the benchmark is expected to gap lower into Monday's session (official 15-Jun print pending). Yet the physical backdrop remains supportive: inventories keep drawing (~320kt, down ~37% YTD from ~509kt in Jan), ~1.4 days of global primary output and ~92% Russian-origin; the cash-3M backwardation had already compressed from 19-yr highs (~$69 on 08-Jun) to ~$4. Net: expect a lower, more volatile price as the geopolitical premium deflates, cushioned by an unrepaired Gulf supply gap (EGA ~12 mo to restore; ALBA impaired).

alumina.name: Alumina FOB Australia (Fastmarkets)
alumina.src: Latest confirmed index; Q2 oversupply pressuring

alumina_notes: Alumina: ex-China FOB Australia (~$366/t) easing on Q2 oversupply vs softer Platts API region (~$305/t); surplus building on higher production/shipments into subdued demand.
Hormuz reopening (14-Jun deal) reverses the rerouting premium — alumina cargo flows through the Gulf should normalize over coming weeks, easing the logistics distortion that had benefited China.
Bauxite: Guinea's stricter export controls still tighten raw-material supply and add upstream cost pressure — a structural offset independent of the Gulf truce.
Refining: Gulf gas-supply disruption that lifted regional refining costs should ease as the blockade lifts; EGA's Al Taweelah refinery may restart ahead of the smelter.
Net: input-cost picture skews softer post-deal (alumina ample, energy/logistics relief), though Guinea bauxite controls keep a floor under raw-material risk.
net_read: REGIME SHIFT — WAR PREMIUM UNWINDING, PHYSICAL DEFICIT INTACT.  Aluminum's run to a four-year high ($3,855 cash, 02-Jun) was built on the Gulf supply shock and Hormuz blockade; the 14-Jun US-Iran deal (permanent end to military operations, Hormuz reopening, blockade removed; signing 19-Jun, 60-day ceasefire) unwinds the acute geopolitical risk premium and should pressure prices near-term — the last confirmed official print is $3,536 (12-Jun, pre-deal), with Monday's session expected sharply lower. The structural floor, however, holds: EGA's Al Taweelah still needs ~12 mo to restore regardless of the truce, ALBA remains impaired, LME stocks keep drawing (~320kt, ~1.4 days of output, ~92% Russian-origin), and a 3–6 mo lag separates the diplomatic resolution from actual metal. Add a firm USD (hot May CPI +4.2% y/y; ~70% odds of a Dec Fed hike) and rising China exports (+15.5% YoY in May). Expect a lower, more volatile price as the premium deflates. Watch: LME's first post-deal settlements, the 19-Jun signing / ceasefire implementation, EGA/ALBA restart timing, and the USD/Fed path.

feed.text: Aluminum expected to open sharply lower as the Gulf war premium deflates on the US-Iran deal; LME official settlement pending — last confirmed close $3,536 (12-Jun).

US and Iran reach a deal: 'immediate and permanent termination of military operations.' Trump authorizes toll-free reopening of the Strait of Hormuz and removal of the US naval blockade; signing set for 19-Jun in Switzerland under a 60-day ceasefire.

Physical reality check: the truce restores Gulf shipping but not damaged capacity — EGA's Al Taweelah still ~12 mo to restore, ALBA impaired; a 3–6 mo lag separates resolution from metal supply. Structural deficit persists.
Oil/energy risk premium unwinds further (Brent already off ~20% from the 2026 peak on ceasefire optimism); lower energy eases smelting/refining cost pressure and is disinflationary at the margin.
Pre-deal, LME official cash settled $3,536 (+1.1% d/d), 3M $3,532; aluminum had steadied after an ~8% pullback from the 02-Jun 4-yr high.
Cash-over-3M backwardation already compressed to ~$4 from 19-yr highs (~$69 on 08-Jun) — front-end physical stress easing before the truce.
LME inventory drew to 319,925 t (−2,075 d/d), down ~37% YTD; ~92% Russian-origin keeps available units scarce — the deficit is unaffected by the deal.
Hot May US CPI (+0.5% m/m, +4.2% y/y) reinforces ~70% odds of a Dec Fed hike and a firm USD — a near-term cap on $-metals.

news.headline: US-Iran deal (14-Jun): permanent end to military operations, Strait of Hormuz reopened, US naval blockade removed; signing 19-Jun, 60-day ceasefire. Removes the dominant aluminum war premium.
news.source: CBS / NBC / Al Jazeera / NPR / CNN (14-Jun)

news.headline: Truce restores Gulf logistics but not capacity: ~9% of global / ~25% of non-China supply still impaired. EGA ~12 mo to restore Al Taweelah; ALBA still down — structural deficit intact.
Aluminum corrected ~8% from the 02-Jun 4-yr high ($3,855 cash) to $3,536 (12-Jun); the 14-Jun deal is expected to accelerate the downside into Monday.
Cash-3M backwardation compressed from 19-yr highs (~$69) to ~$4 — acute front-end scarcity easing, though total stocks stay low.
LME stocks draw to ~320kt (−37% YTD), ~1.4 days of global output; ~92% Russian-origin. Deficit unaffected by the deal.
Hot May US inflation (CPI +4.2% y/y; PPI +1.1% m/m) keeps the Fed hawkish and USD firm; the deal's oil relief is disinflationary at the margin.
Unwrought Al + semis exports +15.5% YoY to 632kt in May; 5-mo cumulative ~2.69 Mt (+10.4%).
Q2-26 oversupply pressuring FOB Australia alumina (~$366/t); Hormuz reopening normalizes rerouted cargoes.

earnings.headline: Revenue $3.2bn (−7% q/q); net income $425m ($1.60/sh); adj. EBITDA $595m; cash $1.4bn.
earnings.readthrough: Higher Al prices + operational resilience amid Gulf disruption; San Ciprián smelter restart completed Apr 2026. Q2 report due ~mid-Jul.

earnings.headline: Adj. EBITDA NOK 8,668m (vs 9,516m yr-ago); adj. EPS NOK 2.07 (beat, up from 1.63).
earnings.readthrough: Primary Al EBITDA +2.7% YoY as Norwegian smelters reopened; soft alumina prices & stronger NOK; Extrusions outlook cut. Q2 due late Jul.

earnings.headline: Adj. EPS $1.63 (beat $1.56); revenue $649m; net income $327m (incl. $288m Hawesville sale gain).
earnings.readthrough: Higher LME + strong US Midwest premiums; Q2 adj. EBITDA guided $315–335m.

earnings.headline: Net profit $200.3m (+316% YoY); EPS 53 fils; sales −17% on Gulf supply-chain disruption.
earnings.readthrough: LME averaged $3,195/t (+22% YoY) drove profit surge; volumes hit by regional conflict; ~19% capacity in controlled shutdown — restart path improves post-truce.

earnings.headline: Net profit SAR 1.63bn (~$436m, +5.5% YoY); revenue SAR 8.79bn (+3.2%); EBITDA $964m.
earnings.readthrough: Strong pricing across commodities; partly offset by lower phosphate volumes; 2026 capex set >$4bn.

earnings.headline: Aluminium 835kt (+1% YoY); alumina +6%; bauxite −11% (Cyclone Narelle at Weipa/Gove).
earnings.readthrough: Flags Gulf curtailments = 23% of ex-China supply; expects enhanced 2026 deficit; Kitimat/AP60 ramp-up.

earnings.headline: Hillside Al ~538kt (9M, FY26 guide 720kt); Brazil Alumina record 1,060kt (9M); Worsley steady.
earnings.readthrough: Alumina sales −15% q/q on availability/timing; annual guidance intact despite cyclone gas disruption.

earnings.headline: Hindalco PAT ₹5,283cr (+66% YoY); India Al upstream EBITDA margin ~46%; Novelis FY26 adj. EBITDA $1.6bn (−9%).
earnings.readthrough: Record India operations; Novelis pressured by Oswego outage (~$104m) and tariffs (~$143m).

earnings.headline: Net profit guided RMB 5.30–5.59bn (+50–58% YoY).
earnings.readthrough: Strong on higher Al prices; China output near self-imposed 45 Mt capacity cap.

earnings.headline: Underlying profit $6.2bn (+>20%); underlying EBITDA $15.5bn (+25%, 58% margin); interim div 73c.
earnings.readthrough: Copper-led strength; broad base-metals read-across positive for the complex.

earnings.headline: FY25 results; copper & aluminium average prices +9% YoY; zinc metal +4%.
earnings.readthrough: Resilient structural demand for Cu/Al cited; broadly constructive sector tone.

earnings.headline: FY25 net loss $455m (vs $803m profit in 2024) on higher costs & lower output.
earnings.readthrough: Analysts expect stronger H1 2026 on higher prices; Asia pivot amid fractured trade flows.

earnings_note: Earnings are quarterly/episodic; entries show each peer's most recent reported results as of 15-Jun (no new releases since the prior run — Q2 season begins mid-July: Alcoa ~mid-Jul, Norsk Hydro late-Jul). Sources: company releases & SEC/exchange filings, Alcircle, Discovery Alert, Investing.com, Zawya/TradeArabia, GuruFocus.

commercial.text: The 14-Jun US-Iran deal removes the war premium that drove the 4-yr-high rally — expect near-term downside and elevated two-way volatility. Accelerate near-dated realization, ensure LME hedges are in place on priced tonnes before further repricing, and avoid chasing the top with new forward fixed-price commitments. Crucially, do NOT over-hedge forward at distressed levels: the unrepaired Gulf capacity (EGA ~12 mo) and low LME stocks cap the downside. Maximise value-added (billet/slab/PFA) premium capture while it lasts.

The Gulf outage drove MJP Q2 +79.5% q/q (~$350–353) and record US MW premiums; Hormuz reopening will, over time, normalize the premium support. Lock in elevated premiums on renewals and spot allocations NOW, before the physical-tightness lag (3–6 mo) gives way to normalized flows. As a non-disrupted supplier, capture premium share while EGA/ALBA remain impaired.

Ex-China FOB Australia (~$366/t) easing on Q2 oversupply vs softer API region (~$305/t); the truce reverses the Hormuz rerouting premium. If net seller, lock firm ex-China pricing on remaining strength; if buyer for smelting, exploit softer spot windows as Gulf flows normalize.

Hormuz reopening and blockade removal should lower Gulf freight, war-risk insurance and routing risk over the coming weeks — capture the relief on committed cargoes. But sequence the stand-down carefully: implementation runs through the 19-Jun signing and a 60-day ceasefire, so keep contingency routing and demurrage clauses live until normalization is confirmed.

Manage the transition narrative with strategic accounts: physical tightness persists through the lag even as the geopolitical risk recedes. Protect OTIF, keep allocation transparent, and convert reliability through the volatility into relationship equity and premium tolerance.

Track: (1) LME's first post-deal settlements (15-Jun onward) and the scale of the premium unwind; (2) deal implementation — 19-Jun signing, 60-day ceasefire durability; (3) EGA/ALBA restart timelines; (4) USD/Fed path (hot May CPI, Dec decision); (5) China export pace & Section 232 tariffs; (6) Guinea bauxite / alumina balance.

bottom_line: A regime shift, not a collapse. The 14-Jun US-Iran deal unwinds the geopolitical war premium and points to a lower, choppier price near-term — but the unrepaired Gulf capacity, drawing LME stocks and 3–6 month supply lag keep a structural floor under the market. For a reliable Gulf-adjacent producer: lock in elevated premiums and near-dated realization before flows normalize, hedge priced tonnes through the volatility, and avoid over-committing forward at distressed levels.
sources: London Metal Exchange via Westmetall — westmetall.com (LME_Al_cash table, 12-Jun close)
CBS News / NBC News / Al Jazeera / NPR / CNN / Fox (14-Jun-26)
Trading Economics — tradingeconomics.com/commodity/aluminum; Worthwill LME tracker
US Bureau of Labor Statistics (CPI released 10-Jun-26)
S&P Global Platts assessments (Dec-25 / Jan-26 / Feb-26; MJP Q2)
caveats: The 14-Jun US-Iran deal is very recent and still pending its 19-Jun signing under a 60-day ceasefire; implementation/durability risk is high. The headline reopening of the Strait of Hormuz restores logistics but does not repair damaged Gulf smelting capacity.

LME official cash/3M settlements publish with a short lag; the latest confirmed official close is 12-Jun (Westmetall) and pre-dates the deal. Monday 15-Jun's official print and the market's reaction are not yet captured here.

Macro/FX/energy values are the latest confirmed Trading Economics reads and are point-in-time (largely pre-deal); oil and safe-haven prices in particular are likely to have moved post-deal — reconcile to live feeds before use.

Daily physical premium assessments (MJP, CIF Japan, EDU, Rotterdam billets, US Midwest) sit behind PRA paywalls (Platts/Fastmarkets/Argus). Values shown are most recent public anchors / status — reconcile to the desk's licensed feed before pricing.
SHFE front-month is a last-reported reference (~CNY 24,370/t SMM spot, excl-VAT USD equivalent); verify on SMM / SHFE for the live print.
Alumina indices diverge by methodology (Fastmarkets FOB Australia vs Platts API region) — use the index contractually relevant to the trade.
This report is market intelligence, not trading or investment advice. Figures are point-in-time and should be re-validated before commercial commitments.

exec_summary: Aluminum corrected lower through the week, unwinding part of the early-June spike. LME cash settled at $3,536/t on Friday 12 June — down 5.4% from the prior-week close ($3,736/t, 5 June) and well off the four-year high of $3,855/t touched on 2 June — with a midweek trough of $3,489/t on 10 June. A firmer US dollar (DXY near 100) and a robust US jobs report, which restored Fed rate-hike leeway, pressured dollar-priced metals; even so, aluminum holds roughly +20% year-to-date and about +45% year-on-year.

The decisive catalyst was geopolitical de-escalation: on 14 June the US and Iran reached a framework agreement to end the war and reopen the Strait of Hormuz, with President Trump ordering an end to the naval blockade and a memorandum of understanding slated for signature around 19 June. The prospect of restored Gulf flows collapsed the prompt scarcity premium — the cash-to-3M backwardation compressed from +$69/t (Monday) to +$4/t (Friday), near flat and off its 19-year highs. Physical tightness has not yet released, however: LME inventories drew a further ~11 kt to 319,925 t (fresh lows, ~1.4 days of global output), and the supply restart is slow — EGA's flagship plant needs ~12 months to full capacity, ALBA remains partially offline, and Gulf primary output fell to a decade-low ~330 kt in April (-35% YoY). Net read: structurally tight, but the war-risk premium has begun to deflate.

price_table.note: Off 4-yr high $3,855 (2 Jun); wk low $3,489 (10 Jun)
Curve flattening as war-risk premium unwinds
Fresh lows ~1.4 days output; draw continues despite price drop
Q2 oversupply weighing; Platts API region - verify PRA
Q2 ~$351.5/t - ~10-yr high; US Midwest also elevated (S.232)

drivers: US-Iran framework deal (announced 14 June) to end the war and reopen the Strait of Hormuz - Trump ordered the naval blockade lifted, with an MOU due ~19 June and the conflict to formally end within ~60 days. This de-escalation is the dominant new catalyst and began unwinding the war-risk premium, collapsing the cash-3M backwardation toward flat.

Macro turned to a headwind: a firmer dollar (DXY ~100) and a strong US jobs report lifted Fed-hike odds, pressuring dollar-priced metals; Brent held elevated near $93/bbl on residual Gulf risk. US CPI/PPI are the next swing factor.

Physical market still tight despite the sell-off: LME stocks fell to fresh lows of ~320 kt (~1.4 days of global output, ~92% Russian-origin), and the Gulf restart is slow - EGA flagship ~12 months to full capacity, ALBA partially offline, Gulf primary output at a decade-low ~330 kt in April (-35% YoY).
China exports kept filling the gap: unwrought aluminum and products rose ~5.7% MoM to 632 kt in May (five-month cumulative 2.69 Mt, +10.4% YoY), even as domestic output runs near the 45 Mt cap.
Raw materials mixed: Guinea's tighter bauxite export controls add upstream cost pressure, while alumina FOB Australia stayed under Q2 oversupply pressure (softer indices) despite the Gulf disruption.

implications.text: the backwardation collapse and softer outright price reduce the urgency to sell prompt-only; the flattening curve opens a window to layer forward hedges on priced tonnes. Sustain value-added premium capture (billet/slab/PFA) and avoid over-committing forward fixed-price near the highs should de-escalation accelerate the unwind.

regional premiums remain supported (MJP Q2 settlement ~$351.5/t, a ~10-year high; US Midwest elevated on Section 232), but a Hormuz reopening is the principal downside risk to premiums into 2H - prioritise near-term premium-share gains and lock favourable renewal terms before normalisation.

softer ex-China alumina (Q2 oversupply) trims net-seller upside; a reopened Strait would lower freight and war-risk insurance - begin unwinding contingency-routing premiums, re-optimise CIF vs FOB exposure, and keep committed-cargo vessel cover without locking peak war-risk freight.

hold OTIF for strategic accounts through the transition; track the ~19 June MOU signing and blockade-lifting mechanics, EGA/ALBA restart cadence, US CPI/PPI and the Fed, and China export/tariff dynamics as the tells for how fast premiums normalise.

outlook: US-Iran MOU signing (~19 June) and the timing/mechanics of lifting the naval blockade and reopening the Strait of Hormuz - the dominant supply and risk-premium wildcard.
US CPI and PPI prints and Fed commentary - the near-term driver of the dollar and dollar-priced metals after the firmer jobs report.
Whether LME stocks keep drawing and the cash-3M curve holds near flat or re-steepens - the real-time gauge of physical tightness versus de-escalation.
China export pace against the 45 Mt output cap, and any further US Section 232 tariff adjustments shaping regional premiums.
weekly.sources: LME official cash/3-month settlements and warehouse stock via Westmetall; benchmark price, USD/DXY, energy and macro via Trading Economics; US-Iran / Strait of Hormuz developments via CNN, NBC News and wire reports (14 June); China May export data via Kitco/Bloomberg citing GAC customs; alumina via Fastmarkets (FOB Australia) and S&P Global Platts (API region); regional premiums (MJP/CIF Japan, US Midwest) via S&P Global Platts and Fastmarkets; Gulf supply and geopolitics via Argus, Mining.com and Discovery Alert.
weekly.caveat: Note: SHFE, alumina and physical-premium figures are most-recent public anchors; daily PRA assessments (Platts/Fastmarkets/Argus) and SHFE/SMM quotes sit behind licensed feeds and should be reconciled to the desk's licensed data before pricing. This brief is market intelligence, not trading advice.
